comments.text: With GPT-5.4 Pro I've managed to get some partial results that you can view here. There's not much literature around the problem. We answer affirmatively the subexponential half of the problem. The proofs are probabilistic, but the structural inputs are quite different. For $F(n)$ the key observation is that the free rank of a union-closed family is exactly the VC-dimension of a complementary set system. For $f(n)$ we use the fact that every family closed under union and intersection is a finite distributive lattice, hence isomorphic to an ideal lattice $J(P)$ by Birkhoff's representation theorem. A large antichain in $P$ yields a Boolean cube inside $J(P)$, and after ruling out large cubes by random colouring one is left with counting bounded-width posets.

Congratulations! In fact, He, Li, and I also obtained almost the same partial result as yours using GPT about 30 hours ago. But before we had finished checking it carefully, we saw that you had already posted this comment first! The GPT-5.4 Pro chat record can be seen here (the chat screenshot is from 14:01 on March 17 2026, Beijing Time).
So we were running GPT on the same problem at the same time. :) I don't read Chinese unfortunately but if you'll have an English version please post it here to compare.
Okay, this is the English translation of our GPT chat history.
I just asked 5.4 Thinking to compare whether the two proofs of \(F(n)<(1+o(1))^n\) are different, and it said they are essentially the same, but your proof is more precise, so the result is stronger.

Gemini 3.1 Pro claims that your reports are indeed very similar. I had given both pdf to Gemini and asked to find something more general using both documents. The answer was: > Based on the provided documents, it is clear that > they represent two stages of solving the same > mathematical problem (Erdős Problem 1183): an > exploratory, conversational derivation (the chat log) > and the resulting formal mathematical paper. Gemini completes with ...: > The synthesis highlights two primary open questions > for future research: > > * Does $f(n)$ grow strictly linearly, or is the $n \log n$ > scale the correct bound? > > * Is $F(n)$ actually bounded by a polynomial, or > is it a true quasipolynomial?
Standard check says it looks plausible but (of course) not in a completed state.
Congrats. Standard check claims 4 required issues that are likely minor.
